topic: Why I stopped using Redux in new React Native projects
angle: what I use instead and when Redux still wins
content:
I stopped adding Redux to new React Native projects about 18 months ago. Haven't regretted it once.

The turning point: I realized most of the state I was putting in Redux wasn't even client state. It was server state. I was manually caching, normalizing, and re-fetching API responses with thunks. TanStack Query took over. Deleted roughly 200 lines of reducers and action creators overnight.

For actual client state (auth token, theme, active filters, a draft in progress), Zustand covers it in one file. No Provider. No `dispatch`. No action type constants.

```ts
const useAuth = create((set) => ({
  token: null,
  setToken: (t) => set({ token: t }),
  logout: () => set({ token: null }),
}));
```

That's the whole store. Import `useAuth` and go.

What surprised me: how often I need state outside the React tree. Push notification handlers. Axios interceptors. Navigation guards. Zustand's `store.getState()` and `store.setState()` work without a Provider. Redux was always awkward for this.

Bundle size: Zustand ~1.2KB gzipped. Redux Toolkit + React-Redux ~13KB. On Hermes, every kilobyte counts toward startup.

Redux still makes sense for large teams. RTK Query is solid, and slices keep 10+ engineers writing predictable code. That consistency has real dollar value. If your team already knows Redux and time-travel debugging is part of your workflow, don't rewrite.

But for a new RN project with 1–5 devs? Zustand + TanStack Query is my default. Smaller API surface, simpler mental model, less code between me and shipping.

What dependency did you replace in your React Native setup that made everything simpler?

#reactnative #mobiledev #javascript #typescript
